claim: Tuberculosis-induced granulomas express immune system protein signatures similar to those of the surrounding tissue.

result.claim: Tuberculosis-induced granulomas express immune system protein signatures similar to those of the surrounding tissue.
result.reasoning: The available evidence refutes the claim that tuberculosis-induced granulomas express immune system protein signatures similar to those of the surrounding tissue. Paper ID 3475317, "Inflammatory signaling in human Tuberculosis granulomas is spatially organized," indicates that the centers of granulomas have a "pro-inflammatory environment" characterized by antimicrobial peptides, reactive oxygen species, and pro-inflammatory eicosanoids (prop_1178, prop_1179, prop_1180). This suggests a distinct protein signature within the granuloma, rather than one similar to the surrounding tissue. Furthermore, Paper ID 10963830, "Differential expression of mycobacterial antigen MPT64, apoptosis and inflammatory markers in multinucleated giant cells and epithelioid cells in granulomas caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis," explicitly discusses "differential expression" of mycobacterial antigen MPT64, apoptosis, and inflammatory markers within different cell types (MGCs and ECs) *within* the granulomas (prop_2359, chunk_17514). This highlights heterogeneity and specific inflammatory responses within the granuloma, which would likely differ from healthy surrounding tissue. The search results did not yield any propositions or chunks supporting the idea of similar protein signatures between granulomas and surrounding tissue.
